item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under a cc-compliant license. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: That's not water. Knowledge of chemical classifications under siege by cognitohazard?
item.content:encoded: "SCP-2029-2 displays catalytic properties during this process, converting sulfur and hydrogen liberated from water molecules into additional SCP-2029-2." What the hell? Water molecules strictly consist of hydrogen and oxygen and like other molecules are defined by composition rather than behavior. Did you mean to say that oxygen is being turned into sulfer or that the sulfur is being liberated from a different molecule?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this article raises no interesting questions. i can't tell if it's trying to be funny or not. and all in all, the concept is wholly uninteresting to me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think a basic knowledge of chemistry is required to really 'get' just how anomalous this SCP is. I'd much prefer it if, at the bare minimum, the science parts are interspersed with some less scienc-y parts. That having been said, the ending of the article was pretty good, and really served to fill in the information that I had not fully understood earlier.
Having said that, if the SCP Foundation was real, this is probably what one of its articles would look like. That, combined with the interesting hook, has me liking this SCP. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I stumbled on that as well. Perhaps something like "last chance to enact procedure 'clean plate' before total loss of earth biosphere is inevitable".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's implied that the table represents what would happen without intervention as of each listed interval after loss of containment. It could probably be clarified, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last two entries of the Addendum seem to be in conflict. The Foundation enacts "Clean Plate" and completely sterilizes the 2029-affected region… but then the Earth becomes uninhabitable within a month anyway? So what was the point of "Clean Plate"? Based on the general thoroughness of the article, I'm sure there's a reasonable connection here… but I don't understand what it is, so it could stand to be clearer.

Also, more specifically: how does "Atmosphere is completely contaminated" happen after "Total sterilization and destruction of contaminated region is absolute"? If you've sterilized the contaminated area, there should be nothing left to contaminate anything else; AFAIK that's basically what sterilization means.

item.title: Neutral Vote for now
item.content:encoded: If it was found on Venus i would upvote. Too much specificity in the containment procedures do not mesh well with little to no hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you see a jar of egg on Venus? XD But no its an Earth that COULD become another Venus. Well that's one theory of it anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, is this what happened to Venus?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kudos for the efforts at scientific accuracy, I was very onboard with the tone.

The idea doesn't grab me so much, partially because thing-that-makes-replicating-world-destroying-stuff was done effectively by SCP-505.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I do plan to constantly edit and revise this. I hate having my readers not know what's going on and get lost from the scientific explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems like a lot more science than it is article. I mean, for all I know this could be some really clever and cool concept, but I had an extremely difficult time following this thing. It's super, super wordy, and the scientific explanation lost me completley. I'm not going to downvote because you obviously put a lot of thought into it, and I don't want to bring you down just because I didn't get it, but you really ought to go through and try to make this one a little bit more readable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of curiosity, is the picture of the egg in question?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh.

It's not bad, per se, and it definitely COULD be good, but right now it's got no backstory, and as Rosmaro said, it's very wordy.

Personally, I like the idea, but I seem to be the minority in that, so a good backstory becomes even more important. Maybe including Incident 2029-1 as an addendum would help.

Neutral vote for now, but I'll check back eventually to see if it's better. This could go somewhere with some work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still a few typos, an SCP-xxx in there too. The article is very wordy and i feel that it could be cut down a bit, but it is otherwise written well with a non-generic idea. I think that it could use more hook though, it's not overly interesting. From what i can see the chemistry is all accurate. I'll edit this more later when I've read through it thoroughly

Good luck

Edit: I've read through the article, and i agree with what most people are saying. The chemistry is all accurate to my knowledge, but i still see a few typos and errors. Writer, may I go through and enter subscript where necessary?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it a bit difficult to follow what was going on. The comments helped clarify exactly what this was, but the description didn't quite do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You basically hit the nail on the head on what it is supposed to do. Sorry I didn't get the right hook for it.

I hard trouble writing the idea to hook onto the reader, since it hooks onto me. Mainly because since I have a 20 year old egg sitting in vinegar in my room and I am deathly afraid of what will happen when I open it up.

But no, the goop doesn't melt through stuff. But it is very toxic and dangerous to organic matter.

Acetic Acid is basically used to break down the object, thus assuring that the object will be neutralized. The idea is the pressure of observing the object and cleaning the chamber out every week or so is the focus point. Though I am sorry bout that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The egg releases some incredibly corrosive goop that, from what I gathered, eats through virtually everything. It converts any water it contacts into more of itself, somehow, and causes carbon to rip away from whatever it was previously bonded with when it melts through stuff.
So, if I'm correct in all of that, this egg makes self replicating goo that could potentially turn earth into Venus if enough got out.

Your tone and writing his top notch, but I really don't find this all too interesting at all. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Random thing what does a random thing. All of the chemistry textbook description in the world isn't going to make it interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The science could be sound for all I know, but I didn't find this interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda don't get it. Also, the sentence beginning "Despite an immediate lack of correlation" is a major run-on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed.

And yeah, I was trying to figure out whether or not it should be considered Keter or not. I mean I know the concept that a keter object, EDIT: is hard to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe that you should change the object class to Euclid. Keter means that it actively needs to be contained, and while yes, this is bad, if you left it alone for a while, not much would happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is still an SCP-xxxx left in there.

Otherwise it is a pickled egg that dispenses a extremely hazardous…gloop.

An alright article except the fact that the keter mark bugs me. If all you got to do is lock it up then occasionally burn off the hazardous byproduct, then it feels like euclid to me. It doesn't matter if said byproduct could go end of the world on us if all you have to do is keep it away from biological matter.

item.title: Special Thanks to Echo~
item.content:encoded:
Who helped editing, refining and defining the chemical properties, descriptions and procedures.

And also Esko and Flameshirt for the needed advice.
